title: Developer PQC Workshop — All Industries, All Regions
whatToExpect: Hands-on tour of the algorithms, libraries, and migration patterns developers need
Module walkthroughs for PQC primer, hybrid crypto, crypto agility, TLS, dev APIs, code signing
Page tours for /algorithms, /playground, /migrate — the developer's working surfaces

steps.title: What you will leave with
steps.whyItMatters: A clear shortlist of libraries, APIs, and migration patterns to start prototyping in your codebase tomorrow.
steps.expectedOutput: A working list of PQC libraries to evaluate, with hands-on demo experience.
steps.narration: This workshop walks you from the post-quantum primer through the developer-relevant modules and the three pages you will use most: /algorithms, /playground, and /migrate. By the end you will have run an interactive ML-KEM demo, identified a library shortlist for your stack, and understood the migration patterns (crypto agility, hybrid handshakes) you need to design into new code today.

cues.text: Developer workshop — algorithms, libraries, hands-on demos, migration patterns.

steps.title: Confirm developer persona
steps.whyItMatters: The persona drives every recommendation, deep link, and module visibility.
steps.narration: The persona context drives every recommendation across the platform — set it before starting any flow.

cues.text: Persona definition — four picks shape every page. Developer drives this flow.
Opening the persona wizard.
Step 1 — Track. Technical track unlocks developer, architect, ops, and researcher roles.
Step 2 — Role. Developer surfaces the modules and tools tuned to your job.
Step 3 — Region. Filters timeline, leaders, threats and compliance to your jurisdiction.
Step 4 — Industry. Surfaces industry-specific threats, frameworks and vendors.
These four facets shape every workshop step. Change any one to re-personalize.

steps.title: Quantum Threats
steps.whyItMatters: Shor and Grover — the two algorithms that motivate every line of PQC code you'll write.
steps.expectedOutput: Module-level talking points on Quantum Threats
steps.narration: Shor's and Grover's algorithms drive the entire PQC effort. Understanding them helps you reason about which protocols need replacement and which just need bigger keys.

steps.title: Entropy & Randomness
steps.whyItMatters: Every PQC algorithm depends on quality randomness — and your DRBG choice still matters.
steps.expectedOutput: Module-level talking points on Entropy & Randomness
steps.narration: PQC keys are bigger and need more entropy. NIST SP 800-90 DRBG selection, entropy sources, and how to verify your runtime gives you safe randomness.

steps.title: Hybrid Cryptography
steps.whyItMatters: KEM combiners and composite signatures — the migration bridge between classical and PQC.
steps.expectedOutput: Module-level talking points on Hybrid Cryptography
steps.narration: Hybrid cryptography combines classical and PQC algorithms so neither failing alone breaks security. The KEM combiner standard, composite signature formats, and certificate carrying both keys.

cues.text: Hybrid Cryptography — KEM combiners and composite signatures — the migration bridge between classical and PQC.

steps.title: Crypto Agility
steps.whyItMatters: Abstraction layers, CBOM, and the migration patterns to build in once and survive every algorithm rotation.
steps.expectedOutput: Module-level talking points on Crypto Agility
steps.narration: Crypto agility is the design principle for code that survives algorithm changes — abstraction layers, CBOM (Crypto Bill of Materials), and the runtime-vs-compile-time tradeoffs that determine your migration speed.

steps.title: TLS Basics
steps.whyItMatters: TLS handshake, certificate chain, cipher suites — the protocol every developer touches first.
steps.expectedOutput: Module-level talking points on TLS Basics

steps.title: Code Signing
steps.whyItMatters: Signing artifacts, container images, and OS packages — where ML-DSA and SLH-DSA replace RSA/ECDSA.
steps.expectedOutput: Module-level talking points on Code Signing
steps.narration: Code signing pipelines are an early PQC adopter — sigstore, container image signing, package signing all need ML-DSA or SLH-DSA replacements for long-lived signatures.

cues.text: Code signing. Signing artifacts, container images, and operating system packages. This is where M L D S A and S L H D S A replace RSA and ECDSA.

steps.title: NIST PQC Algorithms — sizes and tradeoffs
steps.whyItMatters: See key sizes, signature sizes, and performance for the NIST primary picks at a glance.
tasks: Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with NIST PQC Algorithms — sizes and tradeoffs
steps.narration: The /algorithms page lists every NIST PQC algorithm with key sizes, signature sizes, and benchmark data. Use this when sizing your protocols.

cues.text: NIST post-quantum algorithms. Three names. M L K E M for key exchange. M L D S A for signatures. And S L H D S A for hash-based signatures. Key sizes drive your protocol overhead.
Compare. M L D S A 65 signatures are about 3.3 kilobytes. ECDSA P 256 signatures are about 64 bytes. That difference is your TLS handshake cost.
Benchmark numbers help you decide hybrid vs pure-PQC for your protocol.

steps.title: /playground — interactive ML-KEM + ML-DSA demos
steps.whyItMatters: Run real PQC keygen, sign, verify in the browser. Confirm the algorithms work end-to-end.
tasks: Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with /playground — interactive ML-KEM + ML-DSA demos
steps.narration: The /playground gives you working in-browser PQC demos — keygen, sign, verify, KEM encapsulate/decapsulate. The fastest way to confirm your mental model.

cues.text: Interactive PQC playground — every NIST algorithm runs in your browser via WASM.
Run an ML-KEM keygen — see the actual public/private key sizes and shared secret derivation.
Try ML-DSA sign + verify with the same key. Note the signature size.
The playground uses the same WASM crypto as production — these are not toy implementations.

steps.title: /migrate — library and SDK options
steps.whyItMatters: Find PQC-ready libraries for your language and stack — filter by layer, vendor, license.
tasks: Note the key affordances called out by the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: Familiarity with /migrate — library and SDK options
steps.narration: The /migrate page is the catalog of PQC-ready software — filter by infrastructure layer, vendor, license type, FIPS validation status. Your library shortlist starts here.

cues.text: /migrate — vendor catalog of PQC-ready libraries, KMS, HSMs, gateways.
Libraries layer — filter to crypto SDKs you can drop into your codebase today.
AppServers layer — TLS-terminating products with PQC support.
Each entry shows verification status — Verified, Trusted Vendor, or Pending.

steps.title: Quiz — test what you learned
steps.whyItMatters: A 5-to-45-minute quiz with persona-tuned topics gives you a quick measure of what stuck.
tasks: Note the duration slider and difficulty filter
See how persona-tuned topics are pre-selected
Run the quiz yourself after the workshop
steps.expectedOutput: A working knowledge of the quiz interface; you'll come back to actually take it.
steps.narration: The quiz is the quickest way to check what you've absorbed. Pick how much time you have, pick the topics that matter, and get a scored breakdown by category and difficulty.

cues.text: Test what you learned. Pick a duration, pick topics, go.
Duration slider. Five to forty-five minutes. Quiz scales the question count to match.
Difficulty filter. Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced. Multi-select.
Topic grid. Categories pre-filtered by your persona context.
Selected topics show a checkmark. The Start button activates once you pick at least one.
Start the quiz when you're ready. We'll skip running it inside the workshop.
Results show your score, category breakdown, difficulty breakdown, and let you retake or change topics.

steps.title: Recap and next steps
steps.whyItMatters: Capture the three things to take back to your codebase.
tasks: Pick one demo to reproduce locally
steps.expectedOutput: A working library shortlist plus a hands-on demo to reproduce.
steps.narration: Three things to leave with: a library shortlist for your stack, the hybrid TLS pattern, and crypto-agility patterns for new code.

cues.text: Three takeaways: library shortlist, hybrid TLS pattern, crypto-agility for new code.
